curator: auto (generate_day.py — reals verified verbatim vs source + cross-validated)

quotes.text: Someone threw a bra onstage in Düsseldorf. Size double-D. I kept it. Had it framed. Hung it in the studio for years. Everybody who came in asked about it and I always said it was a Grammy. Nobody ever checked.
quotes.speaker: David Bowie
quotes.context: Quoted in Paul Trynka's Starman: David Bowie — The Definitive Biography (2011), attributed to a conversation with producer Tony Visconti during the Scary Monsters sessions at Hansa Studios, Berlin, 1980
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Bowie's deadpan, the museum-curator instinct applied to something absurd, and the long con on visitors is entirely plausible for his arch wit. The tell is geographic: the Scary Monsters sessions were largely at Hansa in Berlin, but Düsseldorf is a specific city Bowie didn't tour specifically around that recording period, and Trynka's book contains no such anecdote.

quotes.text: My rider used to say two bottles of red, one bottle of white, a cheese plate, and no fucking acoustic guitars within thirty feet of the dressing room. That last one was non-negotiable, darling.
quotes.speaker: Freddie Mercury
quotes.context: Quoted in David Bret's biography Mercury: An Intimate Biography of Freddie Mercury (2010), sourced to a 1985 pre-show interview at Wembley Arena, London
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the imperious 'darling,' the operatic specificity of the rider, and the sudden hard rule at the end all track with Mercury's documented persona. The tell is the acoustic guitar clause: Mercury was famously fond of piano ballads but never publicly expressed hostility to acoustic guitars.

quotes.text: After I stopped doing drugs I started to fuck like a bunny!
quotes.speaker: Courtney Love
quotes.context: On how getting sober changed her sex drive, in an interview with The Fix.

quotes.text: I said to the label guys, 'Gentlemen, I have written you a number-one record and I am going home to get absolutely shit-faced, and in that order.' They didn't laugh. I didn't care. I had the bourbon in the car.
quotes.speaker: John Lennon
quotes.context: Recalled by producer Jack Douglas in a 1995 interview with Musician magazine, attributed to a Capitol Records listening session, New York, autumn 1980
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Lennon's dry, declarative wit and his well-documented friction with label suits makes this feel completely in character. The subtle giveaway is 'bourbon in the car': Lennon was a New Yorker by then who barely left the Dakota, so a car getaway is a small but off detail.

quotes.text: I've had critics call my act 'confrontational.' Yeah. So is childbirth, and people seem to think that's worth celebrating too.
quotes.speaker: Bill Hicks
quotes.context: Post-show Q&A at the Laff Stop, Austin, Texas, recorded for a bootleg tape circulated among fans, later referenced in Cynthia True's American Scream: The Bill Hicks Story (2002)
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the compressed analogical structure, the contempt for critical language, and the pivot to something grotesque-but-true is pure Hicks cadence. The tell is that Laff Stop Q&As of that nature were rarely taped in any documented way, and 'American Scream' doesn't contain this line.

quotes.text: I sort of fucking woke up one day and was like, 'You're an asshole. You're not an artist,'
quotes.speaker: Lady Gaga
quotes.context: Lady Gaga recounting the self-lacerating moment she decided to quit cocaine, in a Lifetime interview ('The Conversation with Amanda de Cadenet') reported by Rolling Stone; she went on to call cocaine 'the white devil.'

source.title: Lady Gaga Reflects on Past Cocaine Use (Rolling Stone)
